summary: Round-1 fixes land cleanly. Both blocking-tier majors are fully resolved (AppModule.provideSanctumDatabase now wraps the first Room build() inside the try block and captures the renameTo() outcome with a delete-fallback on refusal; the log line reflects the real filesystem result). All four non-optional minors are addressed as well: the redundant outer launch around warmupDefault() is gone, sweepZombieChats failure logs via the new SWEEP_LOG_COMPONENT = "history-write", and SanctumApplicationTest now has a positive-path testHousekeepingRunsInMainProcess plus symmetric zero-call assertions in the :crash-process test. The open modifiers on WarmupCoordinator and SettingsMigrationHelper are a minimal production-surface concession that the team (including test-reviewer) chose to accept in lieu of extracting dedicated interfaces — acceptable given the KDoc justifies it and the test-reviewer's 'defer to interface later' note. Bonus: @Volatile on AppCorruptionState.corruptionOccurred is a correct preemptive fix for a cross-thread visibility gap that round-1 did not explicitly flag. No new blockers, no regressions.

suggestions.suggestion: The three sidecar deletes (`-journal`, `-wal`, `-shm`) are duplicated verbatim across the rename-success branch (lines 77-79) and the rename-refused branch (lines 86-88). A small private helper `deleteSidecars(parent: File)` would collapse six lines into two call sites and make the next sidecar addition a one-place edit. Not a blocker — the duplication is intentional and readable inside the `when`, and the method body is short enough that the DRY violation is marginal — but worth noting for when the sidecar list grows.
suggestions.benefit: One-place edit when SQLite adds/removes sidecar extensions (e.g., if Room ever switches to a different journal mode). Slightly reduces the chance that a future fix patches one branch and forgets the other.

suggestions.suggestion: `warmupCoordinator.warmupDefault()` is now called BEFORE `appScope` is constructed (line 40), which is fine — warmupDefault uses its own internal scope — but the ordering reads as "warmup, then create the app scope, then launch migration/housekeeping into the app scope", which is a little surprising. Consider reordering so the `val appScope = ...` declaration is the first thing inside the guard block and all three launches follow it (even though warmupDefault doesn't strictly need appScope, grouping the three cold-start collaborators together matches the tech-spec's `Cold start sequence step 1/2/3` narrative). Purely readability — no behaviour change.
suggestions.benefit: Reader can map the three lines 1:1 against the tech-spec's cold-start step 1/2/3 without mentally reordering them.
